title: Testing for gases — how to identify hydrogen, oxygen, CO₂ and chlorine
examTip: Learn the four gas tests as test → result pairs. The examiner wants the exact observation, not just the gas name.
intro: Most gases are invisible and odourless — you can't just look at a gas and know what it is. That's a real problem in the lab when you need to confirm whether a reaction has produced what you expected. These four gas tests are the standard toolkit for identifying the most common gases you'll meet in GCSE chemistry. They're quick, distinctive, and — once you know them — unforgettable. Examiners love them in 'describe a test to identify' questions.

keyPoints.heading: Why we test for gases — and the AQA four
keyPoints.content: Gases are mostly colourless and many are odourless — they can't be identified by sight. Standard chemical tests give distinctive, reliable results that can be used to confirm the identity of a gas. AQA requires you to know the test and the expected positive result for four gases: hydrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide and chlorine. These four gases appear frequently as products in GCSE reactions — in electrolysis, acid reactions, decomposition and combustion. Knowing what to expect (and how to confirm it) is a key practical skill and a reliable source of exam marks.
writeBullets: Gases are colourless — cannot be identified by appearance alone
Standard tests give distinctive positive results that confirm identity
AQA four: hydrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide, chlorine
These appear as products in electrolysis, acid reactions, decomposition and combustion
Exam questions ask: 'describe the test and the result you would expect'
Always describe both the test AND the observation for full marks

keyTerms.def: A standard chemical procedure to identify a specific gas by a distinctive observation

keyPoints.memTip: H-O-C-C: Hydrogen, Oxygen, Carbon dioxide, Chlorine — four tests, four marks.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Reinforce early that the exam wants test + result, not just the gas name. Practice writing both parts every time.

keyPoints.heading: Test for hydrogen — the squeaky pop
keyPoints.content: To test for hydrogen gas: hold a lit splint at the mouth of a test tube containing the gas. If hydrogen is present, it ignites and reacts rapidly with oxygen in the surrounding air, producing a small explosion heard as a characteristic 'squeaky pop' sound. The hydrogen burns: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. This is the definitive test for hydrogen — no other common lab gas produces this sound. Hydrogen is produced in several key reactions: metals reacting with acids (e.g. Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂), the electrolysis of water (at the cathode), and the electrolysis of dilute acids.
writeBullets: Test — hold a lit splint at the mouth of the test tube
Result — a squeaky pop sound (hydrogen ignites with oxygen in air)
Equation — 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O (hydrogen combustion)
Produced by — metals + acids, electrolysis of water (cathode), electrolysis of dilute acids
Distinctive — no other common lab gas produces a squeaky pop
Safety — hydrogen is flammable; use only small amounts in a test tube

keyTerms.def: The sound produced when a lit splint ignites hydrogen gas at the mouth of a test tube
The negative electrode in electrolysis — where hydrogen is produced from water or acid

keyPoints.memTip: H for Hydrogen, H for HOO-ray (the pop). Lit splint → bang.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students sometimes say 'it makes a noise' — insist on 'squeaky pop' as that is the accepted term in mark schemes.

keyPoints.heading: Tests for oxygen and carbon dioxide
keyPoints.content: Oxygen test: Insert a glowing splint (not a burning one — blow it out first) into a tube of the gas. In oxygen, the splint relights. Oxygen supports combustion — it provides the oxidant for the splint to reignite. Oxygen is produced by: decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂, catalyst: MnO₂), electrolysis of water (at the anode), and photosynthesis. Carbon dioxide test: Bubble the gas through limewater (calcium hydroxide solution, Ca(OH)₂). CO₂ reacts with limewater to form a white precipitate of calcium carbonate, turning the solution milky/cloudy white: Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O. If excess CO₂ is bubbled through, the precipitate redissolves (CaCO₃ + CO₂ + H₂O → Ca(HCO₃)₂, which is soluble).
writeBullets: Oxygen test — glowing splint relights when placed in a tube of oxygen
Oxygen supports combustion — provides oxidant for the splint to burn again
Oxygen produced at — anode during electrolysis of water; from H₂O₂ decomposition
CO₂ test — bubble gas through limewater (calcium hydroxide solution)
Positive result — limewater turns milky/cloudy white (calcium carbonate precipitate forms)
Excess CO₂ — white precipitate redissolves as soluble calcium hydrogencarbonate forms

keyTerms.def: A splint that has been blown out but still has a glowing ember — used to test for oxygen
Calcium hydroxide solution — turns milky/cloudy white in the presence of CO₂
The positive electrode in electrolysis — where oxygen is produced from water

keyPoints.memTip: Glowing → grows (relights) in oxygen. CO₂ makes limewater go cloudy — like fog.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Emphasise 'glowing' not 'burning' for the oxygen test — a burning splint test is for hydrogen (it produces the pop).

keyPoints.heading: Test for chlorine — bleaching damp litmus
keyPoints.content: To test for chlorine gas: place a piece of damp litmus paper (red or blue) into a tube of the gas. The paper first turns red (chlorine dissolves in water to form HCl and HOCl — both acidic), then the paper is bleached white (hypochlorous acid, HOCl, is an oxidising bleaching agent that destroys the dye in the litmus). The paper must be damp — dry litmus paper shows no colour change with chlorine because chlorine must first dissolve in water to form HOCl. Chlorine is a pale yellow-green gas with a distinctive sharp, choking smell — always handle in a fume cupboard. It is produced at the anode during electrolysis of brine (sodium chloride solution): 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻.
writeBullets: Test — damp litmus paper (red or blue) placed in a tube of chlorine gas
Result — paper turns red first (acidic), then bleached white
Why damp — chlorine must dissolve in water to form HOCl (the bleaching agent)
Dry litmus — shows no colour change; always use damp paper
Chlorine appearance — pale yellow-green gas; sharp, choking smell
Produced at — anode during electrolysis of brine (NaCl solution)

keyTerms.def: Moistened litmus paper used to test for chlorine — turns red then bleaches white
The bleaching agent formed when chlorine dissolves in water
Concentrated sodium chloride (NaCl) solution — electrolysis produces chlorine at the anode

keyPoints.memTip: Chlorine = bleach in a tube. Damp litmus goes red then white — like a tiny bleaching experiment.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The 'damp' requirement is a classic exam trap — one mark often lost here. Also: the paper goes red THEN white, in that order.

tables.title: The four gas tests — AQA required
rows: Hold a lit splint at the mouth of the tube
H₂ ignites and reacts with O₂ in air: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O
Insert a glowing splint into the tube
Oxygen supports combustion — reignites the splint
Hold damp litmus paper in the tube
Cl₂ + H₂O → HCl + HOCl; HOCl bleaches the dye

tables.title: Where these gases are produced in GCSE reactions
rows: Metal + acid; electrolysis of water/dilute acid (cathode)
Decomposition of H₂O₂ (MnO₂ catalyst); electrolysis of water (anode); photosynthesis
Combustion of carbon/hydrocarbons; acid + carbonate; respiration
Electrolysis of brine (NaCl solution) at the anode

1.question: You hold a lit splint at the mouth of a test tube containing an unknown gas. You hear a squeaky pop. What does this tell you?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! A squeaky pop when a lit splint is applied is the definitive test for hydrogen.
1.wrongFeedback: A squeaky pop is the unique positive result for the hydrogen test. Oxygen relights a glowing splint; CO₂ turns limewater milky; chlorine bleaches damp litmus.

2.question: Why must the litmus paper be damp when testing for chlorine?
options: Chlorine must dissolve in water to form HOCl, which is the bleaching agent
Dry litmus changes colour too quickly and gives a false positive
Chlorine only reacts with wet surfaces due to its charge
Damp paper makes the colour change easier to see
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Chlorine dissolves in water to form hypochlorous acid (HOCl), which bleaches the litmus. Dry paper shows no reaction.
2.wrongFeedback: The key reason is chemistry: Cl₂ must dissolve in water (on the damp paper) to form HOCl, the actual bleaching agent. Dry litmus doesn't change colour at all.

3.question: A gas turns limewater milky white when bubbled through it. What is the gas?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! CO₂ reacts with Ca(OH)₂ to form a white precipitate of CaCO₃, turning limewater milky.
3.wrongFeedback: Limewater turning milky is the positive test for CO₂. The reaction is: Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃↓ + H₂O. Sulfur dioxide does not do this (it would need a different test).

commonMistakes: Using a burning splint instead of a glowing splint for the oxygen test — the test requires a glowing (not burning) splint
Using dry litmus paper for the chlorine test — it must be damp or there is no colour change
Saying limewater turns 'white' rather than 'milky' or 'cloudy white' — the precipitate makes it look milky, not solid white
Confusing which electrode produces which gas in electrolysis — hydrogen is at the cathode (negative), oxygen and chlorine are at the anode (positive)
Forgetting to say the litmus paper turns red THEN white for chlorine — both steps matter and the order matters
examTips: Always write the test AND the positive result — one without the other gets zero marks in a 'describe a test for' question
For the limewater test, use the phrase 'turns milky/cloudy white' — avoid just saying 'white' as the change is a cloudiness, not a solid colour
If asked about chlorine, mention the paper must be damp — this detail is worth a mark and is frequently missed
revisionCardBullets: Hydrogen — lit splint → squeaky pop (H₂ ignites with O₂ in air)
Oxygen — glowing splint → relights (oxygen supports combustion)
CO₂ — bubble through limewater → turns milky/cloudy white (CaCO₃ precipitate)
Chlorine — damp litmus → turns red then bleached white (HOCl bleaches dye)
Chlorine test — paper MUST be damp; dry litmus shows no change
Hydrogen produced — metal + acid; electrolysis of water/acid (cathode)
Oxygen produced — H₂O₂ decomposition; electrolysis of water (anode)
CO₂ produced — combustion; acid + carbonate; respiration
Chlorine produced — electrolysis of brine (NaCl) at the anode
Squeaky pop — diagnostic for hydrogen; no other common gas does this
Excess CO₂ through limewater — precipitate redissolves (Ca(HCO₃)₂ forms)
Chlorine — pale yellow-green gas; choking smell; handle in fume cupboard
